posts.cooked:
What are your favorite second wines worth seeking out? I have never had the luck of experiencing a First-Growth bordeaux (edit: or premier grand cru classe A, etc), and probably won’t have the funds to procure myself a bottle in the near future, but I still love a delicious bottle of well made claret. I know theres been recent posts about inexpensive Bordeaux but what I’m after more I think is:

“what will get me closest to the experience of the legends of Bordeaux without the $500+ price tag?”

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: A couple follow-up questions to frame possible responses. By invoking First Growth, are you restricting the question to the Left Bank or are you including St. Emilion and Pomerol at price points below say Petrus, Ausone, Cheval Blanc, and Lafleur? Also do you like traditional style claret or more modern?

Jayson Cohen:

A couple follow-up questions to frame possible responses. By invoking First Growth, are you restricting the question to the Left Bank or are you including St. Emilion and Pomerol at price points below say Petrus, Ausone, Cheval Blanc, and Lafleur? Also do you like traditional style claret or more modern?

I would say my goal would be to err on the side of less limitation on people’s responses. I’d rather experience fantastic wine than have a significant geographical limit (also, I love right and left bank wines). Probably First Growth is wrong and more like less-accessible is more accurate. Stylistically, though, I would say traditional would be my goal. modern claret-styles can come from all over the world, but what I’m after is wines that myself and those drinking them with me can have and be able to say, THIS is what Bordeaux from great terroir should be.

thanks for your followup questions! hopefully my responses make sense!

posts.name: Henry Kiichli
posts.cooked:
Hi Matt,

There have been a few threads on this, so try the search.

I am still enjoying the 03 la demoiselle de sociando mallet.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Skip second wines and buy wines like Pontet Canet not the second wine of a producer that was designated a First Growth 150 years ago based on price.

Henry Kiichli:

Hi Matt,

There have been a few threads on this, so try the search.

I am still enjoying the 03 la demoiselle de sociando mallet.

searching for “second wines” was a fairly unproductive search, as it just gives me any post with the word “second” in it, but I did try, and sorted through the first few pages of results before giving up. I will for sure seek out the demoiselle!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I don’t think it would be controversial to say that Les Forts de Latour is the best second wine produced in Bordeaux, and it does give you a good taste of what Latour is all about. But I would rather drink Grand-Puy-Lacoste.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Here’s two for each of the main AOCs that probably fit your description at least for a 2014 vintage purchase:

Pomerol: Vieux Chateau Certan, Trotanoy
St. Emilion: Canon, Belair Monange
Pessac-Leognan: La Mission Haut Brion, Haut Bailly
St Estephe: Calon-Segur, Montrose
St Julien: Ducru Beaucaillou, Leoville Barton
Margaux: Palmer, Giscours
Pauillac: Pichon Lalande, Grand Puy Lacoste

I would drink these before second wines of First Growths. Happy exploring!!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Mattstolz:

What are your favorite second wines worth seeking out? I have never had the luck of experiencing a First-Growth bordeaux (edit: or premier grand cru classe A, etc), and probably won’t have the funds to procure myself a bottle in the near future, but I still love a delicious bottle of well made claret. I know theres been recent posts about inexpensive Bordeaux but what I’m after more I think is:

“what will get me closest to the experience of the legends of Bordeaux without the $500+ price tag?”

Ducru Beaucaillou,Leoville las Cases, Pichon Lalande, Palmer, Mission Haut Brion. Try 2016 Pichon Lalande.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: I don’t think that second wines are the best route to your goal. I’ve been fortunate enough to have had quite a few first growths through the years and in almost every vintage there are other wines that equal or surpass most or all of the first growths. I’d look for good vintages from producers like Montrose, Leoville Barton, Palmer, Ducru Beaucaillou (from 1990 forward), Pichon Lalande, Leoville las Cases, Gruaud Larose (between 1978 and 1990, and Talbot, Meyney, and Cantemerle from the same period), Calon Segur, Grand-Puy-Lacoste, and Lynch Bages for a totally non-exhaustive list.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Are second wines the worst value in the wine world?

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Russell Faulkner:

Are second wines the worst value in the wine world?

I wouldn’t go that far. Carruade and Les Forts are probably over-priced but I’d rather drink them than a long list of wines that cost at least that much.

Kris Patten:

Skip second wines and buy wines like Pontet Canet not the second wine of a producer that was designated a First Growth 150 years ago based on price.

If i could “like” posts on here like on Facebook, I would like this comment. thanks!

William Kelley:

I don’t think it would be controversial to say that Les Forts de Latour is the best second wine produced in Bordeaux, and it does give you a good taste of what Latour is all about. But I would rather drink Grand-Puy-Lacoste.

those are probably both good things to know for someone like me who is less experienced on the board. it seems like a lot of the comments agree with you.

Jayson Cohen:

Here’s two for each of the main AOCs that probably fit your description at least for a 2014 vintage purchase:

Pomerol: Vieux Chateau Certan, Trotanoy
St. Emilion: Canon, Belair Monange
Pessac-Leognan: La Mission Haut Brion, Haut Bailly
St Estephe: Calon-Segur, Montrose
St Julien: Ducru Beaucaillou, Leoville Barton
Margaux: Palmer, Giscours
Pauillac: Pichon Lalande, Grand Puy Lacoste

I would drink these before second wines of First Growths. Happy exploring!!

I do love some Canon. i appreciate that most of these are pretty available too. thanks!

Mike Evans:

I don’t think that second wines are the best route to your goal. I’ve been fortunate enough to have had quite a few first growths through the years and in almost every vintage there are other wines that equal or surpass most or all of the first growths. I’d look for good vintages from producers like Montrose, Leoville Barton, Palmer, Ducru Beaucaillou (from 1990 forward), Pichon Lalande, Leoville las Cases, Gruaud Larose (between 1978 and 1990, and Talbot, Meyney, and Cantemerle from the same period), Calon Segur, Grand-Puy-Lacoste, and Lynch Bages for a totally non-exhaustive list.

this seems to be the general consensus. seems like that will be the route to go instead: get the better bang for buck with wines that didn’t grab the top spots 150 years ago. haha

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Leoville Las Cases’ second wine “Clos du Marquis”.
Especially the 1986, 1996 and 2000, are very good, and I only have good things to say about them.
They are not the Grand Vin, but close! -And they only cost a fraction of the big brother.
-They are early drinkable(evolved), while the same LLCs still needs many years more to soften.

-Søren.

This is pretty much what I was gonna say, and the recommendations are superb. I would add Conseillante for Pomerol.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Like everyone else, I’ll suggest Les Forts. A bottle of the 1990 last month was superb.

But another name to consider - Dominus’s second label, Napanook.

I think it is lovely and worth considering if you are looking in the same quality league as Bordeaux’s top deuxiemes.

Pretty much
